planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost risk passes; a potato-leaved variety that benefits from deep planting and very sturdy staking as the large fruits make vines top-heavy.

directions: Start seed indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 70–80°F and transplant after frost risk passes, burying the stem deeply to encourage rooting; Brandywine is arguably the most celebrated American heirloom tomato, a large pink-red beefsteak type with potato-leaved foliage and 1–2 pound fruits that ripen slowly but reward the patient gardener with extraordinarily rich, complex, sweet-acidic flavor widely considered the gold standard of tomato taste. Stake or cage firmly — the vigorous indeterminate vines grow to 6 feet and heavy fruits weigh branches down without support. Days to maturity are long, making it best suited to long-season gardens; in the south, plant early for spring harvest before summer heat sets in.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; stake or cage firmly as an indeterminate variety; the dusky coloring makes ripeness harder to judge — harvest when the shoulders soften and the fruit yields to gentle pressure.

directions: Start seed indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 70–80°F and transplant after all frost risk has passed into rich, deeply prepared, well-drained soil in full sun; Cherokee Purple is a large beefsteak heirloom tomato with a striking dusky rose to purple-maroon skin, deep reddish flesh with purple-green shoulders, and a rich, complex, smoky-sweet flavor that makes it a standout at the table and at farmers' markets. Harvest when fruits soften slightly at the blossom end and shoulders lose their green tinge, as the unusual coloring can make standard ripeness indicators unreliable. An outstanding variety for cooler summers; in zone 9 plant early for spring harvest.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; this is an indeterminate variety that benefits from robust caging or staking to support the 1–2 pound fruits; excellent disease resistance for an heirloom.

directions: Start seed indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant into fertile, amended soil after frost risk passes; Mortgage Lifter is a legendary American heirloom developed by M.C. Byles of Logan, West Virginia in the 1930s by crossing four large tomato varieties over six years — the resulting tomatoes sold so well at 50 cents each that he paid off his mortgage in six years, giving the variety its unforgettable name. Fruits are very large (1–2 pounds), pink-red, with meaty flesh, few seeds, and a mild sweet flavor excellent for fresh eating and slicing. One of the most reliable and high-yielding large heirloom beefsteak types, with better disease tolerance than many heirlooms.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; darker-colored tomatoes absorb more heat and can sometimes ripen faster in cool seasons; stake firmly — indeterminate and vigorous.

directions: Start seed indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after frost risk passes into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Black Krim is a heirloom beefsteak tomato originating from Crimea (the Black Sea region of Ukraine), producing medium-to-large fruits with a distinctive deep brownish-red to almost black skin with green shoulders, and dark red marbled flesh with a rich, complex flavor that is earthy, smoky, and slightly sweet. One of the most striking and flavorful of the dark or 'black' tomato types. Fruits show their best color in warm weather with cool nights; in hot climates the colors may be less dramatic but flavor remains outstanding. Harvest when the skin darkens significantly and gives slightly to pressure.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; ripeness is tricky to judge by appearance — harvest when the skin softens slightly, shows yellow-green stripes against deeper green, and gives to gentle pressure.

directions: Start seed indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to deeply prepared, fertile soil after all frost risk has passed; Green Zebra is a modern heirloom tomato developed by Tom Wagner in 1983, producing attractive 2–3 inch fruits with distinctive lime-green skin and dark green stripes that warm to yellow-green stripes against chartreuse skin when ripe, with a bright, tangy, acidic flesh that is a refreshing counterpoint to sweeter tomatoes. Excellent sliced fresh in salads, as a garnish, or for green salsa — one of the most visually striking tomatoes available. An indeterminate vine, it tends toward compact growth relative to larger beefsteak heirlooms. Yields prolifically over a long season.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; the large fruit size requires staking even as a paste tomato; prolific producer that benefits from a long growing season.

directions: Start seed indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after frost risk passes; Amish Paste is an heirloom paste tomato from Wisconsin Amish communities, producing large (6–8 ounce) ox-heart to plum-shaped fruits with very few seeds, thick meaty flesh, low water content, and rich concentrated flavor that makes it outstanding for sauces, pastes, and canning. It far outperforms Roma for sauce production due to its greater size while retaining the low acidity and meatiness needed for good paste. Also excellent sliced fresh. An indeterminate vine with a long season; in short-summer climates, start early and use season-extension techniques.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; this determinate variety requires only modest staking; prolific bearer best harvested in quantity for drying or canning.

directions: Start seed indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to full sun after frost risk passes; Principe Borghese is the traditional Italian tomato for sun-dried tomatoes, a compact determinate vine bearing enormous clusters of small (2–3 oz) plum to egg-shaped fruits with thick dry flesh, thin skin, few seeds, and very low moisture content — the characteristics that make it ideal for halving and drying in the sun or dehydrator. The variety's name refers to the Borghese family of Rome who historically grew them on terraced hillsides; the dried product is intensely flavored and chewy. It bears all at once in a concentrated flush, making it excellent for large-batch canning and preserving as well.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes. In zone 9 this is an outstanding choice for both spring and fall seasons; plant a second crop in late July or August for fall harvest. Arkansas Traveler is notably heat-tolerant and continues setting fruit at temperatures where most tomatoes drop blossoms.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost in warm (70–85°F) starting mix; transplant to full sun after frost risk passes; Arkansas Traveler is the quintessential heat-tolerant Southern heirloom, developed at the University of Arkansas and introduced around 1939 as a reliable producer through the oppressive midsummer heat of the South and Gulf Coast that causes most other tomatoes to drop blossoms and go dormant. The indeterminate vines produce medium to large (6–8 oz) pink, globe-shaped fruits with mild, low-acid flavor, thin skin, and very good crack resistance — exactly the qualities that make it a top recommendation for zone 8–10 gardeners frustrated by summer tomato failure. The meaty, juicy flesh has classic sweet tomato flavor without sharp acidity. A dependable heirloom for hot climates: one of the best-adapted tomatoes for gardens where summer heat is the limiting factor.
sources: University of Arkansas Cooperative Extension Service

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9–11 this is an outstanding tomato for both spring and fall production — developed specifically for tropical and subtropical climates where standard varieties fail to set fruit in summer heat. In South Florida and the Gulf Coast, Tropic outlasts other varieties well into summer.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–85°F 6–8 weeks before transplanting; set plants in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil; Tropic tomato was developed at the University of Florida IFAS and released in 1963 specifically for tropical and subtropical climates — the most durable, disease-resistant open-pollinated tomato for zone 8b–12. It continues setting fruit at temperatures where most tomatoes stop, has resistance to fusarium wilt, verticillium wilt, and gray leaf spot (the diseases that devastate most tomatoes in South Florida's humid heat), and produces good-sized (6–8 oz), red, globe-shaped fruits with solid, meaty flesh and good flavor from spring well into summer. For Gulf Coast and Florida gardeners who struggle with tomato diseases and summer heat, Tropic remains the benchmark open-pollinated variety after 60 years because no other variety has combined its disease resistance and tropical performance as consistently.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Porter is an outstanding heat and drought-tolerant tomato for Texas and the Gulf Coast — historically the most widely grown home garden tomato in Texas, it performs reliably in hot, dry conditions where other varieties wilt and fail to fruit.

directions: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; set plants in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Porter tomato is a Texas heirloom introduced around 1897–1900 by the Porter & Sons seed company of San Antonio, developed or selected specifically for the demanding conditions of Texas summers — heat, drought, and alkaline soils that would destroy most Eastern varieties. The small to medium (4–6 oz), pinkish-red, slightly oval fruits grow in clusters and have rich, concentrated flavor enhanced by mild water stress. Porter is genuinely drought tolerant and produces in Texas summer heat when standard varieties quit blooming. Historically the most important garden tomato in Central and South Texas, where generations of families grew it through conditions that would kill Brandywine or Cherokee Purple. For any gardener in the hot, dry interior South or Southwest struggling with summer tomato production, Porter is the essential starting point.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 both a spring and a fall planting are productive — Manalucie's resistance to gray leaf spot makes it particularly reliable in the high-humidity conditions of Gulf Coast summers where this disease devastates susceptible varieties.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–85°F 6–8 weeks before transplanting; set out in full sun, fertile, well-drained soil; Manalucie was developed by the University of Florida in the 1950s and named for the Manalucie experimental farm in Homestead, Florida — one of several important tomato varieties developed by UF plant breeders for subtropical Florida conditions. An indeterminate variety producing large, red, globe-shaped fruits (8–12 oz) with solid flesh, good flavor, and outstanding resistance to gray leaf spot (Stemphylium solani), a fungal disease that is especially destructive in Florida and humid Gulf Coast conditions but for which most tomato varieties have no resistance. Manalucie was for decades the standard large beefsteak-type tomato for Florida home gardens because of this specific disease resistance. It remains valuable today for any gardener in zone 8b–11 who has struggled with leaf spot diseases in tomatoes.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 Walter is an outstanding spring and fall tomato with excellent disease resistance for humid Gulf Coast conditions. Determinate habit means a concentrated, predictable harvest good for canning large batches.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; set determinate plants 2–3 feet apart in full sun, fertile, well-drained soil; Walter tomato was released by the University of Florida in 1963, the same year as Tropic, and quickly became a commercial and home garden staple in the Southeast for its reliable production, disease resistance, and compact determinate growth habit. It produces medium-large (6–8 oz), red, round fruits with smooth skin, firm flesh, and good canning and fresh-eating quality, with resistance to fusarium wilt and gray leaf spot. Walter's determinate habit (which concentrates flowering and ripening into a defined period rather than producing continuously like indeterminate types) makes it efficient for gardeners who want a single large harvest for preserving and canning. Once the standard commercial processing tomato in Florida, it remains an important heirloom for Gulf Coast gardeners who need a reliably productive, disease-resistant, compact-season tomato.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; a spring and fall tomato for zone 9–11. Homestead 24 is one of the most heat-tolerant open-pollinated tomatoes available, developed for the intense summer heat of South Florida where standard tomatoes fail.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; set plants in full sun, fertile soil with good drainage; Homestead 24 is a University of Florida IFAS heirloom bred at the Homestead Experiment Station in Homestead, Florida, selected for heat tolerance and disease resistance under South Florida's extreme subtropical growing conditions. The '24' designation indicates a selection within the Homestead series, the result of many generations of selection for performance in tropical heat and humidity. Determinate plants produce medium (6–8 oz), red, globe-shaped fruits with good flavor, firm flesh, and resistance to fusarium wilt and gray leaf spot. An important open-pollinated variety for zone 9b–12 gardeners who want to produce tomatoes reliably in hot, humid summer conditions where most varieties fail to set fruit — one of the trio of UF heirlooms (with Tropic and Walter) that carried South Florida vegetable gardening through decades before modern disease-resistant hybrids became available.
